The Haymarket Square bombing in Chicago that year, though not caused by the Knights, was blamed on labor radicals and triggered a public backlash that devastated the organization. It was replaced by the more conservative AFL.

Unlike the Knights of Labor, the AFL mostly excluded unskilled workers, women, and Black workers. It became the AFL-CIO in 1955 and remains the largest U.S. labor federation.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Great Depression · New Deal Relief · $300: New Deal program (1933–1942) that employed 3 million young men in national parks and forests planting trees, building trails, and fighting erosion — paying $30 a month with $25 sent home to families.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: The CCC (1933–1942) put 3 million young unemployed men to work in national parks and forests, planting 3 billion trees, building 800 state parks, and constructing trails and fire roads. Workers lived in military-style camps, earned $30 a month (most of which was sent to their families), and gained job skills. The CCC was one of the most popular New Deal programs and is credited with restoring much of the nation's eroded and deforested land.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: The WPA (1935–1943) employed 8.5 million workers at its peak — the largest single employer in U.S. history at that time. WPA workers built LaGuardia Airport, New York's Triborough Bridge, and hundreds of courthouses and post offices. Its Federal Writers' Project employed Zora Neale Hurston and Richard Wright; its Federal Theatre Project brought live drama to millions. The WPA represented FDR's conviction that the government should provide work, not just welfare.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Great Depression · New Deal Relief · $500: 1935 New Deal law creating federal old-age pensions, unemployment insurance, and aid to disabled and dependent children — establishing the foundation of the American social safety net.
questions.explanation: The Social Security Act (1935) created three major programs: old-age insurance (pensions for retired workers funded by payroll taxes), unemployment compensation (temporary income for laid-off workers), and aid to dependent children. Signed by FDR on August 14, 1935, it was modeled on European social insurance programs that had existed for decades. Social Security remains the largest federal program and a lasting legacy of the New Deal the Grade 8 Assessment tests regularly.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

choices: choosing an answer because it uses familiar words
questions.explanation: Recovery — the second 'R' of the New Deal — included the Agricultural Adjustment Act (paying farmers to reduce crops and raise prices) and the National Recovery Administration (setting industry codes for minimum wages and maximum hours). Neither fully ended the Depression, and the Supreme Court struck down the NRA in 1935. Economists now generally agree that WWII military spending, not New Deal recovery programs, finally ended the Depression.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Great Depression · New Deal Reform · $200: New Deal structural changes designed to prevent another Great Depression — including the SEC to regulate stock markets, the FDIC to insure bank deposits, and Social Security.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Reform — the third 'R' of the New Deal — aimed to fix the structural flaws that caused the Depression: weak bank regulation (FDIC), unregulated stock speculation (SEC), and no safety net for the unemployed and elderly (Social Security). These reforms survived after the Depression ended and reshaped American capitalism permanently. The Grade 8 Assessment expects students to explain how New Deal reform changed the role of government in the economy.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Great Depression · New Deal Reform · $300: New Deal agency established in 1933 that insures bank deposits up to a set limit, so depositors do not lose savings if a bank fails — eliminating the bank runs that worsened the Depression.
choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: The FDIC (created by the Banking Act of 1933) insured individual bank deposits, initially up to $2,500. With deposit insurance, there was no longer any reason to panic and withdraw savings at the first rumor of trouble. Bank runs — which had caused 9,000 bank failures in three years — essentially stopped. The FDIC is widely credited as the single most effective New Deal reform; today it insures deposits up to $250,000.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Great Depression · New Deal Reform · $400: New Deal agency created in 1934 to regulate the stock market, prevent fraud, and require companies to disclose financial information to investors — the 'cop on the beat' for Wall Street.
questions.explanation: The SEC (1934) was created to prevent the stock fraud and unregulated speculation that had helped cause the 1929 crash. It required corporations to file honest financial statements with the public, banned insider trading, and gave the government power to investigate and prosecute market manipulation. FDR controversially appointed Joseph P. Kennedy (himself a stock market operator) as its first chair, reasoning it 'takes a thief to catch a thief.' The SEC still regulates Wall Street today.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Great Depression · New Deal Reform · $500: New Deal agency (1933) that built dams along the Tennessee River to control floods, generate hydroelectric power, and bring electricity to rural areas of seven Southern states.
questions.explanation: The TVA transformed one of the most impoverished regions of America — the Tennessee River Valley — by building 16 dams for flood control and hydroelectric power. By the 1940s, TVA electricity had brought modern industry and consumer appliances to rural areas that had never had power. The TVA was the New Deal's most ambitious experiment in regional planning and government ownership of utilities — and is still one of the largest public power companies in the U.S.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: On March 6, 1933 — just two days after his inauguration — FDR declared a four-day 'bank holiday,' closing every bank in America to stop the cascade of bank runs. Federal examiners inspected banks; those deemed sound could reopen; weaker ones were reorganized or closed. FDR explained the action in his first radio 'fireside chat.' When banks reopened on March 13, depositors returned money rather than withdrawing it — a dramatic demonstration of how presidential leadership could restore confidence.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: Totalitarian regimes of the 1930s used secret police, propaganda, censorship, and mass terror to eliminate opposition and control citizens' lives completely. Totalitarianism differed from older forms of dictatorship by aiming not just to control political behavior but to reshape society, culture, and individual identity — a Grade 8 context for understanding WWII's ideological dimension.

questions.prompt: World War II · Causes Abroad · $200: Extreme right-wing authoritarian ideology emphasizing ultranationalism, one-party rule, militarism, and violent suppression of communism and democracy — practiced by Mussolini's Italy (1922) and Hitler's Germany (1933).
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.explanation: Fascism emerged in Italy under Benito Mussolini (who coined the term) and in Germany under Adolf Hitler. It combined extreme nationalism with anti-Marxism, glorification of violence, racial ideology, and total state control. Fascist movements exploited economic crisis, national humiliation (Germany after WWI), and fear of communism to seize power. Understanding fascism as an ideology is essential for Grade 8 students explaining why democracies failed to stop Hitler early.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

choices: describing a topic without explaining why it matters
questions.explanation: Nazism (National Socialism) blended traditional German nationalism with biological racism, blaming Germany's defeat in WWI and economic problems on Jewish people and demanding 'Aryan' racial purity. Hitler used the Depression's mass unemployment and the humiliation of the Versailles Treaty to win power democratically in 1933, then abolished democracy. Nazi racial ideology directly caused the Holocaust — the murder of 6 million Jews and millions of others.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: During the 1930s, Japanese military officers increasingly controlled the government, driven by a belief in Japan's divine mission and need for raw materials (oil, rubber, metals) that Japan lacked. Japan invaded Manchuria (1931), launched full-scale war on China (1937), and signed the Tripartite Pact with Germany and Italy (1940). When the U.S. cut off oil exports to Japan in response to its aggression, Japan planned the Pearl Harbor attack to cripple the U.S. Pacific Fleet.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: World War II · Causes Abroad · $500: British and French policy of the late 1930s of giving Hitler territorial concessions — most infamously at Munich in 1938, where they agreed to let Germany annex Czechoslovakia's Sudetenland — hoping to avoid war.
questions.explanation: At the Munich Conference (September 1938), British PM Neville Chamberlain and French PM Daladier agreed to let Hitler annex the Sudetenland of Czechoslovakia — without consulting Czechoslovakia — in exchange for Hitler's promise of no further expansion. Chamberlain returned to London proclaiming 'peace for our time.' Within six months Hitler seized all of Czechoslovakia; within a year he invaded Poland, starting WWII. Munich became the ultimate example of appeasement's failure.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: World War II · U.S. Before Entry · $100: Series of laws passed by Congress in 1935–37 prohibiting arms sales and loans to nations at war — designed to keep the United States out of another European conflict like WWI.

questions.explanation: The Neutrality Acts (1935, 1936, 1937) reflected isolationist determination not to repeat the path to WWI. They banned arms sales and loans to belligerents and (after the Lusitania lesson) prohibited Americans from traveling on ships of warring nations. But when Germany invaded Poland (1939) and France fell (1940), FDR worked to loosen the acts through the Lend-Lease Act (1941), arguing the U.S. could not afford to let Britain fall.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: World War II · U.S. Before Entry · $200: 1941 law allowing the United States to lend or lease war supplies — ships, planes, tanks, and food — to nations fighting the Axis powers, primarily Britain and the Soviet Union, without immediate payment.
choices: ignoring the source or data in the prompt
questions.topic: World War II · U.S. Before Entry
questions.explanation: The Lend-Lease Act (March 1941) allowed FDR to lend or lease war material to 'any country whose defense the President deems vital to the defense of the United States.' Britain received $31 billion, the Soviet Union $11 billion, and other Allies additional amounts. FDR called America the 'arsenal of democracy.' Lend-Lease ended American neutrality in practice before Pearl Harbor formally brought the U.S. into the war.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: World War II · U.S. Before Entry · $300: August 1941 joint declaration by FDR and British PM Winston Churchill stating Allied war aims: no territorial aggression, self-determination for peoples, free trade, and eventual creation of a world peace organization.

The Charter is historically significant as a statement of democratic principles issued four months before the U.S. officially entered the war.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: World War II · U.S. Before Entry · $400: December 7, 1941 surprise Japanese attack on the U.S. naval base at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, killing 2,403 Americans, sinking 8 battleships, and instantly ending American isolationism.

The next day Congress declared war on Japan 82-0 in the Senate and 388-1 in the House; Germany and Italy declared war on the U.S. three days later. FDR called December 7 'a date which will live in infamy.'
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

Germany and Italy fought in Europe and North Africa; Japan expanded through Asia and the Pacific. They shared totalitarian governments and contempt for liberal democracy. The Axis nations were ultimately defeated by the combined industrial and military power of the Allied coalition: the U.S., Britain, Soviet Union, China, and others.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: The Allied coalition brought together nations with very different systems — American democracy, British constitutional monarchy, and Soviet communism — united by opposition to Nazi and Japanese aggression. The 'Big Three' (Roosevelt, Churchill, Stalin) coordinated strategy at conferences in Tehran (1943) and Yalta (1945). American industrial production — the 'arsenal of democracy' — was essential: the U.S. produced 297,000 aircraft and 86,000 tanks during the war.

Under Supreme Commander Dwight Eisenhower, 156,000 Allied troops landed on five beaches (Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, Sword) while 24,000 paratroopers dropped behind German lines. Despite horrific casualties at Omaha Beach, the Allies established a beachhead. Paris was liberated in August 1944, and Germany surrendered eleven months later.

German forces created a 50-mile 'bulge' in Allied lines and surrounded the 101st Airborne at Bastogne, Belgium. When German General von Lüttwitz demanded surrender, Brigadier General Anthony McAuliffe replied 'Nuts!' Patton's 3rd Army relieved Bastogne; the offensive collapsed, costing Germany 100,000 irreplaceable troops.

questions.prompt: World War II · War in Europe · $400: Nazi Germany's systematic murder of 6 million Jewish people and millions of others including Roma, people with disabilities, Soviet civilians, and LGBTQ+ individuals — carried out through death camps, mobile killing units, and mass shootings.
questions.topic: World War II · War in Europe
questions.explanation: The Holocaust was the Nazi state's deliberate attempt to murder all Jewish people in Europe (the 'Final Solution,' decided at the Wannsee Conference, January 1942). Six million Jews — two-thirds of Europe's Jewish population — were killed, along with 5–6 million others. Death camps like Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, and Sobibor used industrial-scale killing. The Holocaust represents a defining moral test of the 20th century and a Grade 8 standard — students must analyze causes, events, and implications.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: World War II · War in Europe · $500: May 8, 1945 — Victory in Europe Day — when Germany officially surrendered to the Allies, ending World War II in Europe after nearly six years of war.
questions.topic: World War II · War in Europe
questions.explanation: Germany surrendered unconditionally on May 7, 1945 (signed May 8 — V-E Day). Hitler had committed suicide on April 30 in his Berlin bunker as Soviet troops closed in. Allied forces had closed in from east (Soviet Red Army) and west (American, British, Canadian). V-E Day brought massive celebrations across the U.S. and Europe, though the war with Japan continued for three more months.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.topic: World War II · War in Pacific
questions.explanation: Island hopping (or 'leapfrogging'), developed by General Douglas MacArthur and Admiral Chester Nimitz, avoided frontal assaults on heavily fortified Japanese islands. By capturing key islands with airfields — like Guadalcanal, Saipan, and Iwo Jima — and bypassing others (leaving Japanese garrisons to 'wither on the vine'), the Allies advanced to within bombing range of Japan by 1944. The strategy minimized American casualties while steadily cutting Japan's supply lines.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.topic: World War II · War in Pacific
questions.explanation: At the Battle of Midway (June 4–7, 1942), American codebreakers (having cracked Japanese naval code JN-25) knew Japan's attack plans. U.S. dive bombers sank four Japanese fleet carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, and Hiryu — and their experienced air crews in one afternoon. Japan lost its offensive naval capability; the U.S. took the strategic initiative in the Pacific. Midway is the Pacific War's most decisive turning point and essential Grade 8 vocabulary.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: The Manhattan Project, directed by General Leslie Groves and scientific director J. Robert Oppenheimer, operated at secret sites including Los Alamos (New Mexico), Oak Ridge (Tennessee), and Hanford (Washington). At 5:29 AM on July 16, 1945, the Trinity test in New Mexico was the world's first nuclear explosion. The project cost $2 billion (about $28 billion today) and permanently changed warfare, diplomacy, and the relationship between science and government.

The Hiroshima bomb (Little Boy, August 6) and Nagasaki bomb (Fat Man, August 9) destroyed both cities and killed 130,000–226,000 people, many from radiation in the months that followed. Japan surrendered August 15, 1945. The decision to use atomic weapons remains one of history's most debated ethical questions.

The formal surrender was signed on September 2, 1945 aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay, with General Douglas MacArthur presiding. WWII was over: 70–85 million people had died, making it the deadliest conflict in human history. The postwar order — UN, NATO, Cold War — followed directly from it.

questions.explanation: WWII rationing required Americans to use ration books to buy meat, butter, sugar, gasoline, nylon, and other goods needed by the military. It also demonstrated Americans' willingness to sacrifice for the common cause — a contrast with the selfishness that complicated the home front in later conflicts.

questions.explanation: The War Production Board (1942–1945) directed U.S. industrial conversion to war production. Auto factories became tank and aircraft plants; consumer goods factories made weapons and uniforms. By 1944, American factories produced more war material than all Axis nations combined. Henry Kaiser's shipyards launched a Liberty cargo ship every 42 days. The WPB demonstrated U.S. industrial capacity and ended the Depression by putting 19 million people back to work.

Women earned wages, gained job skills, and demonstrated competence in fields previously closed to them. When veterans returned in 1945, many women were pushed out of these jobs — but WWII work experience planted seeds for the women's liberation movement of the 1960s.

About 120,000 people, 62% of them U.S. citizens, lost homes, businesses, and freedom. The Supreme Court upheld incarceration in Korematsu v. United States (1944). In 1988 Congress formally apologized and paid $20,000 to each surviving internee, acknowledging the incarceration as driven by racism, not military necessity.

questions.explanation: The United Nations was founded at the San Francisco Conference (April–June 1945) by 51 nations as a replacement for the failed League of Nations. Its charter established the Security Council (with permanent veto-holding members: U.S., USSR, UK, France, China), the General Assembly, and agencies like UNICEF and WHO. Unlike the League, the U.S. joined and helped lead it. The UN represents a Grade 8 example of the U.S. abandoning isolationism and committing to international engagement.

questions.explanation: At Yalta (February 4–11, 1945), the Big Three made fateful decisions: Germany would be divided into occupation zones; the Soviet Union would enter the Pacific war 90 days after Germany's defeat; and free elections would be held in Eastern Europe (though Stalin would not honor this). Disagreements over Poland's postwar government revealed deep tensions between the Western Allies and the USSR that quickly hardened into the Cold War. FDR died just two months after Yalta.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Foreign Policy · Cold War Origins · $100: Post-WWII ideological, political, and military rivalry (1947–1991) between the United States and the Soviet Union — fought through proxy wars, arms races, and propaganda rather than direct combat.

It was fought without direct military confrontation between the superpowers through an arms race, space race, propaganda, espionage, and proxy wars in Korea, Vietnam, Angola, and Afghanistan. The Cold War shaped every aspect of U.S. foreign and domestic policy for 45 years and ended with the Soviet Union's collapse in 1991.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.explanation: After WWII, only the U.S. and USSR had the combination of nuclear weapons, large conventional military forces, industrial capacity, and global political influence to qualify as superpowers. The U.S. possessed the atomic bomb (until 1949), controlled the Western Hemisphere, and dominated Western Europe and Japan economically. The Soviet Union controlled Eastern Europe and China went communist in 1949. The bipolar superpower competition defined Cold War international relations.

Kennan later said his concept was misapplied — he meant political, not military, containment.

He declared it 'must be the policy of the United States to support free peoples who are resisting attempted subjugation.' Congress approved it. The Truman Doctrine was a dramatic departure from isolationism and established the principle that the U.S. would intervene globally to stop communist expansion — the basis for later Cold War commitments.

questions.explanation: Secretary of State George Marshall proposed the European Recovery Program (June 1947); Congress funded $13 billion over four years. By 1952 the Western European economies had recovered to pre-war output levels. The plan succeeded economically and politically — no Western European nation turned communist. Stalin rejected Marshall Plan aid for Eastern Europe, deepening the division between East and West. It remains the most successful foreign aid program in U.S. history.

NATO remains active today with 32 members, expanded after the Cold War ended to include many former Warsaw Pact states.

The arms race doctrine of 'Mutual Assured Destruction' (MAD) argued nuclear war was deterred by the certainty that both sides would be destroyed — a sobering Cold War logic.

Soviet Premier Khrushchev agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a U.S. pledge not to invade Cuba and secret removal of U.S. missiles from Turkey. The U.S. backed anti-communist governments and guerrillas; the Soviets backed communist movements and governments. Both superpowers risked nuclear war if they fought directly, so they fought through proxies — often with devastating consequences for the countries involved. Angola's civil war, fueled by Soviet and American weapons, lasted 27 years.

Detente eased Cold War tensions but was criticized by conservatives as legitimizing communist regimes; it eroded after the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan (1979).

On December 25, 1991, Gorbachev resigned and the Soviet flag was lowered over the Kremlin. The U.S. emerged as the world's sole superpower.

questions.explanation: Civil liberties have been contested throughout U.S. history: the Espionage Act (WWI) restricted speech; Japanese American incarceration (WWII) violated due process; McCarthyism (1950s) suppressed political dissent; and the Patriot Act (2001) expanded surveillance. The Supreme Court has repeatedly had to balance individual rights against government claims of national security. The Grade 8 Assessment tests students' ability to evaluate when, if ever, civil liberties may be limited and what checks exist on government power.
standardRefs: NYS Social Studies Practices and Regents-style reasoning: evidence, chronology/causation, comparison/context, geography, economics, and civic participation

questions.prompt: Domestic Politics and Reform · Civil Rights Policy · $700: Policies in employment and education designed to remedy the effects of past discrimination by giving preferential consideration to members of historically excluded groups — supported as necessary remedy by some, opposed as 'reverse discrimination' by others.

questions.topic: Domestic Politics and Reform · Civil Rights Policy
questions.explanation: Affirmative action programs, dating from Executive Order 11246 (1965), required federal contractors to take affirmative steps to hire minorities and women. In universities, race-conscious admissions became a flashpoint: Regents of UC v. Bakke (1978) banned rigid quotas but allowed race as a factor; Grutter v. Bollinger (2003) upheld diversity as a compelling interest; but Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard (2023) ruled race-conscious admissions programs at Harvard and UNC unconstitutional. The debate over remedying past discrimination through present policy is a central modern constitutional question.

questions.prompt: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry · $100: Post-Civil War era (1865–1877) when the federal government worked to reunite the nation, define citizenship for 4 million freedpeople, and rebuild Southern governments — before ending with the Compromise of 1877.

questions.topic: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry
questions.explanation: Reconstruction (1865–1877) produced three constitutional amendments (13th, 14th, 15th), the Freedmen's Bureau, and the first biracial democratic governments in the South. Its collapse after 1877 — when federal troops withdrew — allowed Jim Crow laws and racial violence to roll back nearly all gains for nearly 90 years. Reconstruction's successes and failures are foundational to understanding the Civil Rights movement a century later.

questions.prompt: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry · $200: 1868 constitutional amendment that granted citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the United States and guaranteed equal protection under law — overturning the Dred Scott decision and establishing the constitutional foundation for civil rights.

questions.topic: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry
questions.explanation: The 14th Amendment (1868) has three key clauses: citizenship for all born or naturalized in the U.S. (overturning Dred Scott); due process protection against state government; and equal protection of the laws. It became the constitutional basis for Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and nearly all subsequent civil rights rulings. The Grade 8 Assessment expects students to trace the 14th Amendment's impact from Reconstruction through the modern era.

questions.topic: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry
questions.explanation: Jim Crow laws, named for a minstrel character, mandated racial segregation from the 1870s through the 1960s. The Supreme Court upheld them in Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) with the 'separate but equal' doctrine — a legal fiction that Brown v. Board (1954) exposed as false. They were enforced not just by law but by lynching and economic retaliation. The Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Voting Rights Act of 1965 finally dismantled Jim Crow as a legal system.

questions.prompt: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry · $400: Post-Civil War transformation of the U.S. into the world's leading industrial nation by 1900 — powered by steel, railroads, and immigrant labor — creating both enormous wealth and brutal inequality that spawned the Progressive reform movement.
questions.topic: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry
questions.explanation: Between 1870 and 1900, U.S. steel production grew tenfold and the railroad network quadrupled. Industrialization created the Gilded Age fortunes of Carnegie, Rockefeller, and Morgan — and the tenement slums, 16-hour workdays, and child labor that muckrakers exposed. It also drove urbanization, mass immigration, and the labor union movement. The Grade 8 Assessment expects students to connect industrialization to Progressive Era reform as cause and effect.

questions.prompt: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry · $500: Early 20th-century reform movement using muckraking journalism, settlement houses, and government regulation to address the social and economic problems caused by industrialization and corporate monopoly power.
questions.topic: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Reconstruction + Industry
questions.explanation: Progressive reformers tackled child labor (Keating-Owen Act, 1916), unsafe food (Pure Food and Drug Act, 1906), corporate monopolies (Sherman Antitrust Act enforcement), and political corruption (direct election of senators, 17th Amendment, 1913). Constitutional changes included the 16th (income tax), 17th (Senate elections), 18th (Prohibition), and 19th (women's suffrage) Amendments. Progressivism established the model of using government power to regulate capitalism — the template for the New Deal and Great Society.

questions.prompt: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Imperialism + World War I · $500: Woodrow Wilson's proposed international organization to prevent future wars through collective security — approved at the 1919 Versailles Peace Conference but rejected by the U.S. Senate, fatally weakening it.
questions.topic: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Imperialism + World War I
questions.explanation: Wilson's League of Nations was his crowning peace proposal, yet the Senate refused to ratify the Treaty of Versailles twice (1919, 1920), keeping the U.S. out. Without U.S. participation, the League lacked power to stop Japanese aggression in Manchuria (1931), Italian invasion of Ethiopia (1935), or German rearmament (1936). Its failure contributed directly to WWII — and its successor, the United Nations (1945), was designed to prevent the same mistakes by ensuring U.S. membership.

questions.prompt: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Depression + World War II · $100: October 1929 collapse of stock prices on Wall Street — triggered by speculation, margin buying, and investor panic — that wiped out billions in wealth and set off the banking crisis and Depression that followed.

questions.explanation: The stock market crash (Black Thursday, Oct. 24; Black Tuesday, Oct. 29, 1929) was the triggering event of the Great Depression. The Grade 8 Assessment expects students to distinguish the crash (trigger) from deeper causes like overproduction, inequality, and weak bank regulation.

questions.prompt: Grade 8 U.S. History II Yearlong Review · Depression + World War II · $500: Nazi Germany's systematic murder of 6 million Jewish people and millions of others, carried out through death camps like Auschwitz and mobile killing units — representing the most extreme consequence of totalitarianism and antisemitism.

The UN's 1948 Genocide Convention and Universal Declaration of Human Rights were direct responses. Grade 8 standards require students to explain the Holocaust's causes and connect it to the responsibilities of bystanders.
